figures.gloss: The supreme, benevolent creator of the universe. Though acknowledged as the ultimate source of all existence and destiny, he is infinitely distant, having withdrawn from the chaotic, day-to-day affairs of the material world.
figures.facet: Because he is too vast and remote to be moved by human suffering, Vodouists do not invoke him directly in ritual or offer him sacrifices; all active spiritual intercession is strictly delegated to the Lwa.
figures.provenance: Syncretism of French Catholic theology ('Bon Dieu') with West African high gods (Mawu, Olodumare); ethnographic studies by Leslie Desmangles.

figures.gloss: The elder guardian of the cosmic crossroads and the spiritual gates. In every Vodou ceremony, he must be saluted and invoked first, as he holds the singular power to open the barrier between the human realm and Vilokan (the spirit world).
figures.facet: Evolving from the phallic, youthful trickster of West Africa, Haiti's Legba became a frail, limping old man leaning on a crutch—bearing the deep historical trauma and physical scars of the Middle Passage and enslavement.
figures.provenance: Fon and Yoruba oral traditions; syncretized universally with St. Peter or St. Lazarus; Maya Deren's 'Divine Horsemen'.

figures.gloss: The raucous, obscenity-shouting master of the cemetery, death, and regeneration. Dressed in undertaker's tails, dark glasses, and a top hat, he leads the Gede family of spirits, demanding rum infused with hot peppers and dancing with unbridled sexual energy.
figures.facet: He is not a grim, terrifying reaper but an agent of fierce social critique and obscene humor. By mocking the elite and asserting the undeniable truths of sex and death, he acts as the ultimate equalizer in a historically traumatized society.
figures.provenance: Endemic to the Haitian experience, merging Central African (Kongo) ancestral practices with European Masonic and mortuary aesthetics.

figures.gloss: The immense, primordial cosmic serpent whose coiled body holds the earth together and who moves through the waters of creation. Associated with blinding whiteness, peace, and ultimate purity, he represents the oldest layers of existence.
figures.facet: Damballah is so ancient and fundamentally pure that he possesses no articulate speech. When he mounts a horse (initiate), he does not talk; he only hisses, slithers on the ground, and seeks water or raw eggs, embodying an uncorrupted African antiquity.
figures.provenance: Direct descendant of the Dangbé snake cult of Ouidah (Fon); historically syncretized with St. Patrick or Moses.

figures.gloss: The profoundly complex spirit of love, beauty, and womanhood. She manifests most famously as Freda, a hyper-feminine, weeping spirit who adores luxury, and as Dantor, a fierce, dark-skinned, knife-wielding single mother who ruthlessly protects her children.
figures.facet: Erzulie embodies the fractured femininity of the plantation. Freda weeps because her desire for perfection is permanently thwarted by reality, while Dantor's muteness traces to the trauma of the Revolution. Both are avatars of profound historical grief.
figures.provenance: Dahomean water spirits merged with Catholic Mariology; syncretized with Mater Dolorosa (Freda) and the Black Madonna of Częstochowa (Dantor).

figures.gloss: The aggressive, machete-wielding Lwa of iron, warfare, political power, and fire. He is the blacksmith who forged the tools of civilization and the general who leads the defense of the nation against tyranny.
figures.facet: In the Haitian context, Ogou absorbed the specific iconography and ultimate betrayal of the Revolution. To demonstrate that raw military power inevitably destroys itself, he often turns his violence inward, ritually stabbing or burning himself during possession.
figures.provenance: The Yoruba Orisha Ogun and Fon Gu; heavily influenced by the Haitian Revolutionary armies and syncretized with St. James the Greater.

figures.gloss: The physical and spiritual center post of the Vodou peristyle (temple). It serves as the cosmic antenna, the exact vertical pathway along which the Lwa descend from their underwater homeland of Vilokan to enter the bodies of the faithful.
figures.facet: It grounds abstract mythology into tangible architecture. Without this specific, consecrated column of wood or concrete planted in the earth, the geometry of possession collapses; the spiritual world cannot intersect with the physical courtyard.
figures.provenance: Foundational Haitian temple geometry; informed by the Kongo dikenga cosmogram and West African sacred enclosures; Maya Deren's 'Divine Horsemen'.

figures.gloss: The phenomenon of divine possession, literally translating as 'the horse'. In this supreme act of devotion, a human's soul (gwo bon anj) is temporarily displaced so that a Lwa can physically inhabit and pilot their body.
figures.facet: This is an act of total ontological surrender. The human vessel retains absolute amnesia of the event; the Lwa does not merely inspire them, it hijacks their flesh, proving that the gods are physically present and willing to sweat with their people.
figures.provenance: Core praxis of Haitian Vodou; documented extensively by Alfred Métraux in 'Voodoo in Haiti'.

figures.gloss: The foundational categorization of the lwa into two nations. The Rada spirits are ancient, sweet, and cool, hailing directly from Africa; the Petwo spirits are hot, aggressive, and forged in the crucible of New World slavery.
figures.facet: This is not a European dualism of good and evil but a thermodynamic reality of survival: the gentle Rada spirits could comfort the enslaved, but it took the volatile, explosive power of the Petwo spirits to actually break the chains.
figures.provenance: Haitian ethnography and oral tradition (Métraux; Deren; Brown, Mama Lola); the Rada/Petwo division is an organic ritual taxonomy that crystallized over generations of plantation-era practice, not a single founding act — though the 1791 Bois Caïman ceremony is remembered as its most dramatic historical expression.

figures.gloss: Admiral of the ocean and sovereign of its creatures, husband of Lasirenn; honored with the barque of Agwe — a small raft laden with cakes, champagne, and flowers, floated out to sea and sunk as an offering.
figures.facet: Sovereignty performed as departure: Agwe's central rite is watching an offering sail away and vanish beneath the water, making the ocean's rule a domain defined by what is given up rather than what is kept.
figures.provenance: Documented in Haitian Vodou ethnography — Alfred Métraux, Voodoo in Haiti; Maya Deren, Divine Horsemen — the barque ceremony is a standard-described maritime Rada rite.

figures.gloss: The sacred divine twins, saluted immediately after Legba in the standard ritual order; simultaneously children and older than the other lwa, embodying a doubled, capricious, fertile power fed with their own small plates.
figures.facet: Power multiplied by being split: the Marassa's holiness comes precisely from their doubling — two who are treated, fed, and addressed as one undivided sacred unit, this shard's clearest instance of twin-power.
figures.provenance: Documented in Vodou liturgical order and ethnography (Métraux; Karen McCarthy Brown, Mama Lola); the Marassa cult and its distinctive twin-feeding rites are standard in the literature.

figures.gloss: Lwa of the marketplace and of the shredded palm frond, patroness of initiation (kanzo) and, by tradition, the first mambo (priestess); her frond marks and protects the boundary of consecrated ritual ground.
figures.facet: The gatekeeper of legitimacy: without Ayizan's sanction the priesthood has no mechanism to reproduce itself — she is less a deity of miracles than the one who licenses who may perform them.
figures.provenance: Documented in Haitian Vodou ethnography of initiation ritual (Métraux; Brown, Mama Lola).

figures.gloss: Lwa of trees, healing leaves, and the sacred asson (beaded rattle) that marks a fully initiated priest; by tradition the first houngan (priest), paired with Ayizan as the archetypal founding priestly couple.
figures.facet: Authority handed down as an object: Loko's asson is not a symbol of office but the office itself — to be given one, in the kanzo rite, is to become a houngan, making Loko the source of the tradition's whole chain of ordination.
figures.provenance: Documented in Haitian Vodou ethnography of priestly initiation (Métraux; Brown, Mama Lola).

figures.gloss: Serpentine lwa of springs, rain, and magic-workers, of Kongo (bisimbi) lineage; conductor between the waters and patron of those who deal in occult knowledge, invoked at freshwater sites.
figures.facet: The Kongo cult carried across the Atlantic largely intact: Simbi keeps the freshwater/serpent/knowledge cluster of the Kongo bisimbi almost unchanged, one of Vodou's clearest unbroken lines back to a single named African source.
figures.provenance: Continuity with the Kongo bisimbi water-spirits documented in Kongo-Atlantic religious ethnography; the Haitian Simbi cult is described in Métraux and Brown.

figures.gloss: Wife of Baron Samedi and mother of the Gede spirits; the foul-mouthed white lady of the cemetery, said to be embodied by the first woman ever buried in a given graveyard, and a judge among the dead in her own right.
figures.facet: The death-family's missing half restored: where Baron presides over burial, Gran Brijit presides over the ground itself — the cemetery's actual foundation is a woman's grave, not a man's authority.
figures.provenance: Documented in Haitian Vodou ethnography of the Gede/death-lwa family (Métraux; Brown, Mama Lola; Donald Cosentino's work on Gede iconography).

figures.gloss: The straw-hatted, sack-and-machete-carrying peasant lwa of agriculture, crops, and hard marketplace bargaining; a fixture of the possession repertoire and of Haiti's rural self-image.
figures.facet: Divinity styled as the ordinary farmer: Azaka carries no crown or trident, only a peasant's tools and suspicion of townspeople — sanctity here is the dignity of subsistence labor, not power over it.
figures.provenance: Documented in Haitian Vodou ethnography (Métraux; Brown, Mama Lola); Azaka's possession behavior (haggling, hoarding food) is a standard described feature.

figures.gloss: The mermaid lwa, wife of Agwe, who pulls the chosen beneath the water and returns them changed — initiated, clairvoyant, and hers; ties Vodou into the broader Caribbean and Atlantic water-woman complex.
figures.facet: Initiation staged as disappearance: Lasirenn's central myth is abduction, not instruction — those she takes are gone for a time and return transformed, salvation modeled on being taken rather than being taught.
figures.provenance: Documented in Haitian Vodou ethnography (Métraux; Brown, Mama Lola); the wider Afro-Atlantic mermaid/water-woman complex is discussed in comparative Caribbean religious scholarship.

figures.gloss: Intricate ground-drawings, traditionally traced in cornmeal, wheat flour, or ash around the poto-mitan, each pattern the specific signature and landing-point of a given lwa, drawn fresh before major ceremonies.
figures.facet: Summons rendered as geometry: a vèvè is not decoration but an address — the lwa is invited by having its precise sigil redrawn on the ground each time, ritual efficacy resting on the accuracy of a picture.
figures.provenance: Documented extensively in Haitian Vodou ethnography (Deren, Divine Horsemen; Métraux); vèvè-drawing is a standard-described element of ceremony preparation.

figures.gloss: The night ceremony of August 1791, led by the houngan Dutty Boukman and the mambo Cécile Fatiman, at which participants are said to have sworn an oath and made sacrifice on the eve of the uprising that became the Haitian Revolution.
figures.facet: History and liturgy fused into a single founding act: Bois Caïman is remembered simultaneously as a religious ceremony and as the opening scene of national independence, making Vodou's own foundational memory inseparable from Haiti's origin story.
figures.provenance: Haitian oral history and national historiography; treated as a documented-but-legendarily-elaborated event in modern Haitian historical scholarship (e.g. David Geggus's work on the Haitian Revolution).

figures.gloss: Vodou's dual-soul doctrine: the ti bon anj ('little good angel') is the seat of conscience, personality, and individual character, while the gwo bon anj ('big good angel') is the animating life-force temporarily displaced from the body during possession.
figures.facet: A soul built to be borrowed: the doctrine exists specifically to explain possession — only by dividing the self into a part that stays (ti bon anj) and a part that can be lent out (gwo bon anj) does a lwa have anywhere to enter.
figures.provenance: Documented in Haitian Vodou ethnography of possession and the soul (Métraux; Deren; Brown, Mama Lola).

edges.note: Legba holds the metaphysical keys to the gates, but the poto-mitan is the physical highway he opens for the spirits to descend.
The thermodynamic 'temperature' of the Lwa—cool Rada or hot Petwo—determines the violence, rhythm, and physical toll exacted upon the human horse during possession.
GOLD SEAM: A direct historical and theological continuation of the Dahomean crossroad deity, softened into an elder in the Caribbean.
GOLD SEAM: Shares the exact West African origin as the Yoruba gatekeeper, retaining the absolute monopoly over cosmic communication.
GOLD SEAM: The Yoruba lord of iron transplanted intact, though in Haiti he dons a general's uniform to fight the revolution.
GOLD SEAM: Retains the exact Dahomean attributes of war, fire, and the path-clearing machete.
GOLD SEAM: The ancient python of Ouidah, preserving an unbroken, amniotic lineage back to the African source.
GOLD SEAM: Both are primordial, water-associated serpents representing the ancient, continuous flow of life and cosmic architecture.
GOLD SEAM: The Catholic God absorbed completely into the Vodou cosmology as the ultimate, albeit withdrawn, creator.
GOLD SEAM: The Dahomean transcendent creator mapped seamlessly onto the French Catholic God.
GOLD SEAM: Skeletal lords of the dead who fuse macabre terror with a profound, leveling cosmic inevitability.
GOLD SEAM: Both act as the vital vertical axis structurally connecting the underworld, earth, and heavens.
GOLD SEAM: The Congolese cosmogram directly informs the spatial and spiritual geometry of the Haitian peristyle and the intersecting planes of existence.
GOLD SEAM: A fundamental, non-moral dualism mapping the universe into complementary forces—cool/ancient versus hot/kinetic.
GOLD SEAM: Religious systems where the supreme ritual act requires human vessels to be physically inhabited and piloted by the gods.
GOLD SEAM: Complex, tempestuous spirits encompassing the terrifying extremes of both boundless desire and devastating wrath.
GOLD SEAM: Sovereigns of the dead who command immense respect and fear, ruling an inescapable subterranean domain.
GOLD SEAM: In Haiti, Papa Legba took the chromolithographs of St. Peter (the keys) and St. Lazarus (the crutch and dog) as his images — Catholic saint iconography repurposed as lwa portraiture.
Death personified as a skeletal patron of the marginal, paid in rum, cigars, and cigarette smoke — parallel New World figures with no demonstrated common descent.
GOLD SEAM: The mounted body across hemispheres — the deity rides a human vehicle whose own personality withdraws for the duration.
Papa Legba's chromolithograph borrows St. Peter's keys directly — the historical apostle's own iconography, not merely the abstracted sainthood, supplies the lwa's image.
Both describe a living human body permanently or repeatedly 'mounted' by a specific spirit so that the spirit's presence and authority continue to act through it in the world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: both frame the medium's body as a mount or vessel ridden/worn by the possessing spirit.
Husband and wife, jointly sovereign over the sea — the admiral of its surface and the mermaid of its depths, a standard-attested Vodou marriage.
Husband and wife who together head the Gede family and preside over the cemetery — the death-family's roster is husband-and-wife, not husband-only.
The archetypal first priestess and first priest, patrons of the kanzo initiation together — the mechanism by which the tradition's priesthood reproduces itself is jointly held by this pair.
Fixed ritual order: the Marassa are saluted immediately after Legba opens the gate, a standard-documented sequence at the start of Vodou ceremony.

The chwal possession rite is only intelligible through this doctrine: it is specifically the gwo bon anj that is displaced so a lwa can mount the horse, the concept this shard's own possession entry presupposes.
the Haitian Simbi is a direct continuation of the Kongo bisimbi water-spirits, keeping their freshwater/serpent/magician cluster largely intact across the Middle Passage.
Both are Afro-Caribbean mermaid-spirits who claim and transform those they take beneath the water — a shared regional water-woman complex, not a single traceable line of descent.

Both are Kongo-derived ground-drawn sigils summoning a specific spirit to a specific place — Haitian vèvè and Brazilian ponto riscado are parallel New World developments of the same Kongo cosmogram (dikenga) drawing tradition.

Bois Caïman is remembered as the dramatic historical moment at which the already-existing Rada/Petwo ritual taxonomy proved itself in revolutionary action — a symbolic and historical touchstone, not the occasion on which the taxonomy was founded.
parallel diaspora descendant of the same Dahomean serpent complex, developed independently in Haiti.

dossier.essence: An Afro-Haitian religion that crystallized among enslaved West and Central Africans on the plantations of French Saint-Domingue (colonial Haiti) and their descendants, blending Fon, Yoruba, and Kongo traditions with folk Catholicism. It serves a remote high creator, Bondye, through a large pantheon of spirits, the Lwa, who are actively engaged in daily life through offering and possession. It remains widely practiced in Haiti and its diaspora, frequently alongside Catholicism.
dossier.cosmos: Bondye ('Good God') created and sustains the universe but is too vast and distant to petition directly, so all practical dealing is with the Lwa, grouped into 'nanchon' (nations), chiefly the cool, ancient Rada and the hot, fiery Petwo forged in the New World. The ancestors and the dead matter greatly; spirits and the dead are said to dwell in Ginen (mythic Africa) beneath the waters, and a person's souls (notably the gwo bon anj and ti bon anj) are ritually managed at and after death.
dossier.practice: Worship happens in a temple (ounfo) around its central post (poto-mitan) under priests and priestesses (oungan and manbo), through drumming, dance, song, veve ground-drawings, animal offering, and above all possession, in which a Lwa 'mounts' a devotee like a horse (chwal) to speak and act among the living. Lwa are identified with Catholic saints, and service is reciprocal: the Lwa are fed and served, and in return give protection, healing, and counsel. The 1791 Bwa Kayiman (Bois Caiman) ceremony is remembered as igniting the Haitian Revolution.
dossier.canon: An oral tradition with no scripture, carried in songs, prayers, ritual language (langaj), and initiatory knowledge; documented ethnographically by Alfred Metraux, Maya Deren, and later scholars.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds the god present in the flesh, divinity that descends to inhabit and speak through the worshipper's own body, within a moral universe reworked out of the trauma of slavery.
sources: Maya Deren, 'Divine Horsemen: The Living Gods of Haiti' (1953)
Leslie G. Desmangles, 'The Faces of the Gods: Vodou and Roman Catholicism in Haiti' (1992)
Karen McCarthy Brown, 'Mama Lola: A Vodou Priestess in Brooklyn' (1991)
